blocks:
Ayahs

إِنَّآ أَعْطَيْنَـٰكَ ٱلْكَوْثَرَ (١)
Indeed We have given you abundance. (1)
فَصَلِّ لِرَبِّكَ وَٱنْحَرْ (٢)
So pray to your Lord, and sacrifice [the sacrificial camel]. (2)
إِنَّ شَانِئَكَ هُوَ ٱلْأَبْتَرُ (٣)
Indeed it is your enemy who is without posterity. (3)

(Commentary)

This Surah is an expression of favor toward the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) by granting him the Abundance (الكوثر), and a consolation for his noble soul (تطييب لنفسه الشريفة) by declaring that his hater is the one who is cut off (الأبتر). It is the shortest Surah in the Qur’an. Reports differ as to whether the Surah is Meccan or Medinan, though it appears to be Meccan. Some have mentioned that it was revealed twice to reconcile the various reports.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا أَعْطَيْناكَ الْكَوْثَرَ (Indeed We have given you the Abundance. [108:1]) Al-Tabarsi said in al-Majma‘: "The word al-Kawthar (الكوثر) follows the morphological pattern of faw‘al (فوعل), referring to something whose nature is characterized by multiplicity (الكثرة); thus, al-Kawthar means 'abundant good' (الخير الكثير)." End quote.

Opinions have differed regarding the interpretation of al-Kawthar to a remarkable degree. It has been said to be: abundant good; a river in Paradise; the Pond (الحوض) of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in Paradise or at the Place of Gathering (المحشر); his children; his companions and followers (أشياعه) until the Day of Resurrection; the scholars of his community (أمة); the Qur’an and its many virtues; the Prophethood (النبوة); the facilitation of the Qur’an and the lightening of religious laws; Islam; monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts; knowledge and wisdom (الحكمة); his virtues (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله); the Praiseworthy Station (المقام المحمود); or the light of his heart (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), among other things. It has been reported from some that they counted up to twenty-six different opinions.

The first two opinions rely on certain narrations, while the remaining views are not free from arbitrary imposition (تحكم). Whatever the case may be, His saying at the end of the Surah: إِنَّ شانِئَكَ هُوَ الْأَبْتَرُ (Indeed your enemy is the one who is cut off. [108:3])—where the apparent meaning of al-abtar (الأبتر) is one whose progeny is severed—indicates that the sentence is a form of "inversion of the claim" (قصر القلب). This suggests that the multiplicity of his progeny (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is specifically intended by the "Abundance" (الكوثر) granted to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), or that it refers to "abundant good" (الخير الكثير) with the multiplicity of progeny being included within that abundant good. Were it not for this, the substantiation of the discourse with His saying, "Indeed your enemy is the one who is cut off," would be devoid of benefit.

Numerous reports (استفاضت الروايات) state that the Surah was revealed concerning those who taunted him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) for being "cut off" (البتر) after the death of his two sons, al-Qasim and ‘Abdullah. This refutes the claim that the hater (الشانئ) meant by "cut off" (الأبتر) one who is severed from his people or severed from good, and that Allah responded by saying he is the one severed from all good.

Because His saying, "Indeed We have given you," is an expression of favor (امتنان) toward him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), the first-person plural (المتكلم مع الغير) was used to denote grandeur (العظمة). And since it serves to console his noble soul, the sentence was emphasized with inna (إن) and expressed with the verb "giving" (الإعطاء), which signifies the granting of ownership (التمليك).

The verse is not without indication that the children of Fatimah (عليه‌السلام) are his progeny (ذريته) (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). This is itself one of the miraculous predictions (ملاحم) of the Noble Qur’an, for Allah, the Exalted, multiplied his progeny after him to such an extent that no other lineage can equal them, despite the calamities (النوائب) that befell them and the widespread massacres (المقاتل الذريعة) that decimated their ranks.

His saying, the Exalted: فَصَلِّ لِرَبِّكَ وَانْحَرْ (So pray to your Lord, and sacrifice. [108:2]) The apparent context (السياق) of branching the command for prayer and sacrifice (النحر) from the favor mentioned in His saying, "Indeed We have given you the Abundance," is that it is an act of gratitude for the blessing (شكر النعمة). The meaning is: "Since We have favored you by giving the Abundance, then be grateful for this blessing through prayer and sacrifice."

According to what both schools (الفريقان) have narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and from ‘Ali (عليه‌السلام), and what the Shi‘a have narrated from al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) and other Imams, the meaning of al-nahr (النحر) is raising the hands during the takbir (تكبير) of the prayer up to the throat (النحر).

It has also been said that the meaning of the verse is: "Pray the ‘Id prayer for your Lord and sacrifice the camels (البدن)." It has also been said to mean: "Pray to your Lord and stand straight (استو قائماً) when raising your head from bowing (الركوع)." Other interpretations have also been mentioned.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ شانِئَكَ هُوَ الْأَبْتَرُ (Indeed your enemy is the one who is cut off. [108:3]) The hater (الشانئ) is the one who harbors animosity (المبغض), and the "cut off" (الأبتر) is the one who has no offspring (عقب). This hater was al-‘As ibn Wa’il.

It has been said that "cut off" (الأبتر) means severed from good or severed from his people, but you have already seen that the reports regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) do not align with this, as will be seen.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Bukhari, Ibn Jarir, and al-Hakim narrated via Abu Bishr from Sa‘id ibn Jubayr from Ibn ‘Abbas that he said: "The Abundance (الكوثر) is the good that He gave him." Abu Bishr said: "I said to Sa‘id ibn Jubayr: 'Some people claim it is a river in Paradise.'" He replied: "The river in Paradise is part of the good that Allah gave him."

In the same source, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Hakim, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in his Sunan narrated from ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه‌السلام) that he said: لما نزلت هذه السورة على النبي صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله «إِنَّا أَعْطَيْناكَ الْكَوْثَرَ» قال النبي صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله لجبريل : ما هذه النحيرة التي أمرني بها ربي؟ قال : إنها ليست بنحيرة ـ ولكن يأمرك إذا تحرمت للصلاة ـ أن ترفع يديك إذا كبرت ـ وإذا ركعت وإذا رفعت رأسك من الركوع ـ فإنها صلاتنا وصلاة الملائكة الذين في السماوات السبع ، وأن لكل شيء زينة ـ وزينة الصلاة رفع اليدين عند كل تكبيرة. — "When this Surah, 'Indeed We have given you the Abundance,' was revealed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to Gabriel: 'What is this sacrifice (النحيرة) that my Lord has commanded me with?' He replied: 'It is not a sacrifice; rather, He commands you, when you enter the state of sanctity (تحرمت) for prayer, to raise your hands when you say the takbir, when you bow, and when you raise your head from bowing. For this is our prayer and the prayer of the angels in the seven heavens. Indeed, everything has an adornment, and the adornment of prayer is raising the hands at every takbir.'"

The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: رفع اليدين من الاستكانة التي قال الله : « فَمَا استَكانُوا لِرَبِّهِمْ » — "Raising the hands is part of the humble submission (الاستكانة) of which Allah said: 'But they did not humble themselves before their Lord...'"

I say: This was also narrated in al-Majma‘ from Muqatil from al-Asbagh ibn Nubatah from him (عليه‌السلام). He then said: "Al-Tha‘labi and al-Wahidi recorded it in their commentaries." He also stated that all of his (the Prophet's) pure progeny (عترته الطاهرة) narrated from him (عليه‌السلام) that the meaning of al-nahr (النحر) is raising the hands to the throat in prayer.

In the same source, Ibn Jarir narrated from Abu Ja‘far (عليه‌السلام) regarding His saying: فَصَلِّ لِرَبِّكَ (So pray to your Lord) — "It is the prayer"; وَانْحَرْ (and sacrifice) — "He said: He raises his hands at the very first takbir of the opening."

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas regarding His saying: فَصَلِّ لِرَبِّكَ وَانْحَرْ (So pray to your Lord, and sacrifice) — "He said: Allah revealed to His Messenger: 'Raise your hands level with your throat (نحرك) when you say the takbir for prayer; that is the nahr (النحر).'"

In al-Majma‘, regarding the verse, from ‘Umar ibn Yazid who said: "I heard Abu ‘Abdullah (عليه‌السلام) say regarding His saying: فَصَلِّ لِرَبِّكَ وَانْحَرْ — 'It is raising your hands level with your face.'"

I say: He then said: "‘Abdullah ibn Sinan narrated the same from him, and something similar was narrated from Jamil from him (عليه‌السلام)."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Sa‘d and Ibn ‘Asakir narrated via al-Kalbi from Abu Salih from Ibn ‘Abbas who said: "The eldest of the Messenger of Allah's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) children was al-Qasim, then Zaynab, then ‘Abdullah, then Umm Kulthum, then Fatimah, then Ruqayyah. Al-Qasim died in Mecca, being the first of his children to die, then ‘Abdullah died. Al-‘As ibn Wa’il al-Sahmi then said: 'His progeny is severed; he is cut off (أبتر).' So Allah revealed: إِنَّ شانِئَكَ هُوَ الْأَبْتَرُ (Indeed your enemy is the one who is cut off.)"

In the same source, al-Zubayr ibn Bakkar and Ibn ‘Asakir narrated from Ja‘far ibn Muhammad from his father (عليهما السلام) who said: "Al-Qasim, the son of the Messenger of Allah, passed away in Mecca. As the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was returning from his funeral, he passed by al-‘As ibn Wa’il and his son ‘Amr. When al-‘As saw the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), he said: 'I surely hate him.' Al-‘As ibn Wa’il then said: 'No doubt, he has become cut off (أبتر).' So Allah revealed: إِنَّ شانِئَكَ هُوَ الْأَبْتَرُ."

In the same source, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from al-Suddi who said: "The Quraysh used to say, when a man's male children died: 'So-and-so has been cut off (بتر).' When the son of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) died, al-‘As ibn Wa’il said: 'He is cut off (بتر),' and the abtar (الأبتر) is the one who is solitary (الفرد)."

I say: In some reports, the hater (الشانئ) is said to be al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah; in others, Abu Jahl; in others, ‘Uqbah ibn Abi Mu‘ayt; and in others, Ka‘b ibn al-Ashraf. However, the relied-upon view is what was mentioned previously.

This is supported by what is in al-Tabarsi's al-Ihtijaj from al-Hasan ibn ‘Ali (عليه‌السلام) in a discourse where he addressed ‘Amr ibn al-‘As: وأنك ولدت على فراش مشترك ـ فتحاكمت فيك رجال قريش منهم أبو سفيان بن حرب ـ والوليد بن المغيرة وعثمان بن الحارث ـ والنضر بن الحارث بن كلدة والعاص بن وائل ـ كلهم يزعم أنك ابنه ـ فغلبهم عليك من بين قريش ألأمهم حسبا ـ وأخبثهم منصبا وأعظمهم بغية. ثم قمت خطيبا وقلت : أنا شانئ محمد ـ وقال العاص بن وائل : إن محمدا رجل أبتر لا ولد له ـ قد مات انقطع ذكره ـ فأنزل الله تبارك وتعالى : «إِنَّ شانِئَكَ هُوَ الْأَبْتَرُ» — "And you were born on a shared bed, and the men of Quraysh disputed over you—among them Abu Sufyan ibn Harb, al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah, ‘Uthman ibn al-Harith, al-Nadr ibn al-Harith ibn Kaldah, and al-‘As ibn Wa’il—each claiming you were his son. Then the one among them with the meanest lineage, the foulest position, and the greatest transgression prevailed over them. Then you stood as an orator and said: 'I am a hater (شانئ) of Muhammad.' And al-‘As ibn Wa’il said: 'Muhammad is a man who is cut off (أبتر), having no son; once he dies, his mention will cease.' So Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, revealed: 'Indeed your enemy is the one who is cut off.'" (The narration continues).

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: Regarding إِنَّا أَعْطَيْناكَ الْكَوْثَرَ — "He said: al-Kawthar is a river in Paradise that Allah gave to Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in exchange for his son Ibrahim."

I say: This report, despite being disconnected (مرسل) and having an unidentified source (مضمر), contradicts other narrations. Furthermore, interpreting al-Kawthar as a river in Paradise does not conflict with interpreting it as "abundant good," as previously mentioned in the report of Ibn Jubayr.
